text: The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the colon and dissecting the mesocolon off Drota's fascia. The ureter is identified, and the mid-ureter is clipped but not divided to prevent tumor cell migration into the bladder. The fourth robotic arm provides traction on the ureter, enhancing exposure in the pelvis.

The surgeon dissects the ureter into the pelvis until encountering the detrusor fibers, indicated by the widening of the ureter as it enters the bladder. The bladder mucosa is entered, and a barbed suture is anchored to the bladder opening. The bladder cuff is sequentially released with scissors and sutured to prevent retraction. The ureteral orifice is identified, and the bladder cuff is completely released. The cystotomy is closed in two layers using the barbed suture, and the bladder is filled to ensure a watertight closure.

The surgeon focuses on the kidney, isolating the renal vein and artery. The renal artery and vein are divided using an endoscopic stapler. The dissection plane between the upper pole of the kidney and the adrenal gland is developed, preserving the adrenal gland in situ. The posterior and lateral attachments of the kidney are divided, allowing the kidney to be released.

The surgeon performs retroperitoneal lymph node dissection by isolating the periaortic lymphatics, starting at the renal hilum and moving inferiorly. Polymer clips are used to control the lymphatic tissue surrounding the aorta, and the inferior extent of the lymphatic packet is secured with additional clips. The lymphatic packet is then placed into an endoscopic retrieval bag.
